title: Vanilla fudge
subtitle: Soft, creamy fudge beaten to a smooth set
excerpt: Thirty-six pieces of vanilla fudge: caster sugar, full-fat condensed milk, butter, and milk cooked to 116°C (soft ball stage), then beaten off the heat until the fudge thickens and loses its gloss, poured into a lined tin, and left to set. The beating stage determines the final texture.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the cream candy section documents a sugar and cream confection cooked to the soft ball stage (when a drop of syrup forms a soft, malleable ball in cold water), then beaten until set. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the sugar confection chapter documents the soft ball stage as the correct stopping point for cream-based sweets that should set soft and smooth. Vanilla fudge using condensed milk and butter is documented in British domestic confectionery from the Edwardian period onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut pieces in a bag layered with baking paper for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fudge keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target: soft ball stage (116°C) — use a sugar thermometer

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-based, deep saucepan — the mixture boils and foams.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point in sugar cooking when the syrup reaches 112–116°C. To test without a thermometer: drop a small amount of syrup into a cup of cold water. At soft ball stage, the syrup forms a soft, pliable ball that flattens between your fingers when pressed. Below this temperature, the syrup is too soft to set; above it, the sugar will crystallise too fast during beating and produce a grainy rather than smooth fudge. A sugar thermometer is more reliable than the cold-water test.

content.text: Fudge is a two-stage process: cook to temperature, then beat until set. The cooking stage brings the sugar to the

at 116°C. The beating stage (done off the heat) forces the sugar to crystallise in tiny crystals that give fudge its smooth, melt-in-the-mouth texture. Beat too little and the fudge stays sticky. Beat too long and it sets in the saucepan before you can pour it into the tin.

attrs.title: Hot sugar is extremely dangerous
attrs.body: Boiling sugar syrup reaches over 116°C and sticks to skin. Keep children away from the hob during cooking. Use a deep saucepan to prevent splashing. If any syrup splashes onto skin, run immediately under cold water for at least 10 minutes. Do not stir with your finger at any point.

content.text: Grease and line a 20 × 20 cm tin. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{condensed-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large, heavy-based saucepan over low heat. Stir continuously until the sugar has dissolved and the butter has melted — do not let it boil before the sugar dissolves or the fudge will be grainy. Once dissolved, bring to a steady boil over medium heat, stirring constantly to prevent the mixture catching on the base. Cook, stirring, until the temperature on a sugar thermometer reads 116°C. This takes 15–20 minutes.

Remove from the heat and stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Leave to cool for 5 minutes without stirring. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon for 8–12 minutes until the fudge thickens, loses its gloss, and becomes lighter in colour — the moment you notice it is difficult to stir and has taken on a slightly grainy texture, pour it immediately into the prepared tin. Work quickly.

Smooth the surface with the back of a spoon. Score into 36 squares while still warm — the fudge will crack if you try to cut it once fully set. Leave at room temperature for 2 hours to set completely, then cut along the score lines and remove from the tin. Store in an airtight tin, separated by baking paper.
